hoses: Hydrant supply hose from hydrant to Engine 181 intake
Ladder supply line A: 200 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to ladder truck intake
Ladder supply line B: 200 ft of 2½-inch hose from Engine 181 discharge to ladder truck intake
nozzles: Aerial ladder pipe master stream, 750 GPM @ 80 PSI
appliances: Ladder pipe nozzle at the tip of the aerial ladder
sceneElements.elevation: Ladder pipe nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181, using elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft

problems.title: Total Ladder Pipe Flow
problems.question: The ladder pipe is supplied by two equal supply lines. Each supply line is carrying 375 GPM to the ladder truck. What known ladder pipe flow is being supplied?
problems.instructorExplanation: The two supply lines combine to feed one ladder pipe. Add the equal line flows to find total ladder pipe flow.
problems.explainMistake: Do not count the ladder pipe as an additional separate flow beyond the water carried in the two supply lines.

problems.title: Flow Per Ladder Supply Line
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies the ladder truck with two equal supply lines. The ladder pipe is flowing 750 GPM total. What flow should be used for each ladder supply line?
formulaBreakdown: Flow per line = total ladder pipe flow / number of supply lines.
problems.instructorExplanation: The two ladder supply lines split the total flow evenly. Each line carries 375 GPM.
problems.explainMistake: Do not calculate friction loss using 750 GPM in each line. Each supply line carries half of the total ladder pipe flow.

problems.title: Ladder Supply Line Friction Loss
problems.question: Each ladder supply line is 200 ft of 2½-inch hose. The ladder pipe flow is 750 GPM split equally between two supply lines. Use FL = C × (GPM/100)² × (length/100), with C = 2. What is the friction loss in each supply line?
formulaBreakdown: FL = C x (GPM / 100)^2 x hose length in hundreds.
problems.instructorExplanation: Each supply line carries 375 GPM. At 375 GPM through 200 ft of 2½-inch hose, friction loss rounds to 55 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss from both equal supply lines together. Calculate one supply path because the lines are parallel.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Elevation Pressure
problems.question: The ladder pipe nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181. Use elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. What elevation pressure must be added?
formulaBreakdown: Elevation pressure = elevation height x 0.5 PSI per foot.
problems.instructorExplanation: The ladder pipe is above Engine 181, so elevation pressure must be added. A 60 ft elevation gain requires 30 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not subtract elevation pressure for an elevated ladder pipe. Pressure must be added for uphill elevation.

problems.title: Ladder Pipe Pump Discharge Pressure
problems.question: Engine 181 supplies a ladder truck with two equal 200 ft 2½-inch supply lines. The ladder pipe flows 750 GPM total; split that flow equally between the two supply lines for friction-loss calculations. The ladder pipe nozzle pressure is 80 PSI, the nozzle is 60 ft above Engine 181, and ladder waterway appliance loss is 25 PSI. Use C = 2 for each 2½-inch supply line and elevation pressure = height × 0.5 PSI/ft. What pump discharge pressure should Engine 181 set?
formulaBreakdown: PP = FL + NP + elevation + appliance loss.
Friction loss per supply line = 55 PSI.
problems.instructorExplanation: For a ladder pipe supplied through two equal lines, calculate one supply path. Add supply line friction loss, ladder pipe nozzle pressure, elevation pressure, and ladder waterway appliance loss. Engine 181 should pump 190 PSI.
problems.explainMistake: Do not add the friction loss of both blue supply lines together. Parallel supply lines split the flow, so one supply path governs the pump discharge pressure.
